responses.strategic_rationale: Establishing a 3-year residential NREM parasomnia unit in Bonn requires a careful balance among data richness, participant burden, safety, and long-run sustainability. The core tensions center on capturing infrequent parasomnia events within a naturalistic home-like environment while maintaining rigorous clinical standards, managing budget and staffing for extended stays, and preserving privacy in public-facing data while enabling robust benchmarking of semi-automated event triage. The 6 levers below operationalize these competing priorities through controlled escalation of data collection intensity, targeted recruitment strategies, privacy governance, optimized staffing, validated automation workflows, and stage-gate expansion decisions to enable iterative learning and credible publication trajectories.

levers.name: Tiered sensing escalation protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever directly shapes data richness versus participant burden. An adaptive escalation improves data quality for events but increases scheduling complexity and staffing demands; if escalations fail due to sensor downtime or false negatives, data gaps may arise and equity across participants can be affected.
options: Maintain nightly low-burden monitoring across all participants to minimize intrusiveness while collecting core data.
Adopt a fixed schedule of enhanced nights for all participants to standardize data quality across the cohort.
Implement an adaptive escalation protocol triggered by detected events to allocate full PSG nights only when clinically warranted.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing data richness with participant comfort and privacy. A weakness the options miss: escalation triggers may fail due to sensor downtime or subtle events that do not trigger a full PSG, creating data gaps.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — how to escalate data collection approach

levers.name: Recruitment and retention optimization
levers.consequences: This lever shifts enrollment pace and data completeness. Narrow pre-screening can raise event yield per participant but narrows generalizability; higher compensation benefits retention but increases total cost and risk to budget; flexible scheduling improves throughput but adds logistical complexity and potential biases in stay timing.
options: Tighten pre-screening to favor participants with higher likelihood of events, accelerating data yield.
Increase per-night compensation and meals to improve retention across extended stays.
Implement rapid-contact follow-up and flexible scheduling to optimize recruitment throughput while preserving study integrity.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing representativeness with event yield and cost. A weakness the options miss: tighter pre-screening may reduce external validity and slow recruitment from broader patient populations.
levers.lever_classification: execution — recruitment and retention optimization

levers.name: Data privacy and sharing governance
levers.consequences: Defining data sharing posture lowers privacy risk and protects participants, but may constrain external collaboration and replication. The chosen approach influences long-term data reuse, auditability, and cross-study benchmarking, with potential trade-offs in rapid reproducibility and external validation.
options: Publish de-identified sensor data openly to accelerate cross-study benchmarking under data use agreements.
Enforce internal-only access for all raw data with no external release beyond de-identified aggregates.
Implement a tiered access model with independent reviews for requests involving sensitive bedroom video, allowing exceptions under governance review.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: privacy versus openness and reproducibility. A weakness the options miss: regulatory constraints or inter-institution data-sharing complexities that could stall collaborations.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how data privacy and sharing policy is governed

levers.name: Staffing and safety coverage optimization
levers.consequences: Aligning staffing with 8 active suites affects safety, wakefulness, and data throughput. Cross-training reduces headcount but may dilute specialized expertise; outsourcing non-core tasks can free time but may introduce data-handling risks and SLA dependencies. Overall, throughput and safety margins improve but with potential quality and cost implications.
options: Adopt a rotating night-shift schedule with one dedicated safety officer per shift to ensure coverage across 8 suites.
Cross-train technicians to handle data annotation and participant monitoring to reduce headcount.
Outsource non-core tasks like data labeling to external partners with clear SLAs to free internal time for scoring.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: maintaining safety and throughput within budget. A weakness the options miss: cross-training or outsourcing can impact data integrity if not tightly governed or if external partners lack domain-specific training.
levers.lever_classification: operations — staffing and safety coverage optimization

levers.name: Semi-automated event triage workflow
levers.consequences: Adopting semi-automation aims to reduce manual review burden while preserving reliability through human adjudication. Over-reliance on automation risks missed nuance or bias in labeling; without ongoing calibration, performance may drift as data accumulate. The approach should improve efficiency and consistency but requires ongoing validation and governance.
options: Implement semi-automated event triage with algorithmic ranking combined with mandatory human adjudication.
Develop automated scoring scripts to assign event labels with external expert review to calibrate the system.
Adopt a blinded, double-scoring protocol with pre-registered adjudication rules to maximize reliability.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: efficiency gains from automation versus risk of algorithmic bias or drift. A weakness the options miss: the need for continuous calibration data and performance audits to prevent degradation over time.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — data analysis workflow and triage automation

levers.name: Expansion gate decision criteria
levers.consequences: Expansion proceeds only after meeting safety, data quality, and throughput milestones, mitigating overcommitment. Alternatively, parallel expansion or maintaining footprint preserves momentum in one area but may fragment resources or delay publishable results. This lever directly links growth to measurable outcomes and safeguards compliance.
options: Authorize expansion from 8 to 12 suites only after reaching predefined data quality, safety, and throughput milestones.
Proceed with expansion in parallel across two cohorts with separate funding streams to diversify recruitment.
Maintain current footprint and reallocate funds to intensify data analysis and publication efforts rather than physical expansion.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: growth ambition versus operational risk and resource limits. A weakness the options miss: external funding shifts or regulatory timelines could constrain milestone achievement, untested in current plan.
levers.lever_classification: execution — expansion gating and scaling decisions

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia unit operates at the intersection of intensive in-situ observation, longitudinal phenotyping, and scalable data-driven triage tooling. Core tensions arise between participant autonomy and data richness, between rigorous clinical monitoring and resource constraints, and between open scientific collaboration and strict privacy. The six levers below are designed to lock in governance, optimize data collection methodology, ensure safe operations, and accelerate dissemination without compromising safety or privacy. Collectively they address recruitment tempo, data quality, staffing resilience, and scholarly impact, while preserving the plan’s domestic-appearance emphasis and strict de-identification standards.

levers.name: Consent and ethics oversight cadence
levers.consequences: These measures deepen participant autonomy and regulatory alignment, but they may slow enrollment if re-consent steps are triggered too aggressively. They also shift administrative workload toward a dedicated liaison, which reduces staff time for other tasks. Opt-out data pathways can limit data richness but improve trust and retention.
options: Implement a two-stage consent process with initial broad consent followed by a targeted re-consent during weeks 1 and 4 to reaffirm data usage preferences and scope changes.
Appoint a dedicated ethics liaison to coordinate monthly protocol amendments, consent updates, and site-level audit readiness across Bonn and University oversight.
Provide opt-out pathways for non-essential data streams while preserving core monitoring for safety and data integrity, with documented participant preferences.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: autonomy versus operational speed; weakness: options do not explicitly address Bonn-specific regulatory timing or language support that can affect consent flow.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how consent and ethics oversight cadence is structured

levers.name: Enhanced night-scan scheduling strategy
levers.consequences: Fixed enhanced nights guarantee a predictable data yield but strain resources if events are sparse. Conditional escalation conserves manpower but risks missing subtle events if signals are ambiguous. Randomized night allocation improves phenotyping generalizability but adds staffing complexity and may delay care workflows.
options: Adopt a fixed plan of three enhanced PSG nights per participant at weeks 0, 4, and 8 to maximize event capture while preserving safety and schedule feasibility.
Implement a conditional escalation: only trigger enhanced PSG when preliminary low-burden streams indicate suspicious patterns warranting clinical-grade resolution.
Incorporate randomized assignment of enhanced PSG nights across study participants to eliminate scheduling bias and improve generalizability of longitudinal phenotyping results.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: schedule rigidity versus adaptive sensitivity; weakness: options do not fully address weekend staffing variability or local hotel/clinic constraints that affect night coverage.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — which data collection approach to use

levers.name: Floor plan and safety policy adaptation for privacy and throughput
levers.consequences: Modular privacy increases participant comfort and reduces observation intrusion, improving recruitment and retention. It may raise construction and maintenance costs and require ongoing staff training. Flexible spaces can speed data collection during high-event nights, but require strict cleaning, safety checks, and consistent camera/audio coverage to avoid gaps.
options: Implement modular room partitions and adjustable privacy zones to balance unobtrusive observation with participant comfort during parasite episodes.
Adopt enhanced corridor safety protocols and staff quick-access routes to minimize response times during high-priority events.
Reconfigure common spaces into flexible observation lounges that can be converted for PSG setup while ensuring domestic ambience remains.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: privacy versus throughput; weakness: options may not explicitly address acoustic treatment quality or camera coverage consistency across reconfigured spaces.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to sequence the physical layout and safety adjustments

levers.name: Cross-site data integration protocol
levers.consequences: Standardization improves reproducibility and accelerates collaboration while preserving privacy; federated approaches reduce data transfer risk but require robust security architecture. Tiered access speeds legitimate research but can introduce delays for time-sensitive investigations and require ongoing governance.
options: Adopt EEG-BIDS aligned data pipelines with automated metadata tagging and version control across all sensors to ensure reproducibility.
Implement a federated data-sharing framework that allows external researchers to perform secure in-situ analyses without transferring identifiable data.
Establish a tiered data-access policy with rapid review for approved investigators and longer compliance checks for data-intensive projects, prioritizing safety.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: openness versus privacy; weakness: options may not fully address Bonn-specific legal constraints on cross-border data transfers and local IRB timing.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — which data collection or data handling approach to use

levers.name: Staffing risk mitigation and shift optimization
levers.consequences: Multiskilled staffing reduces single-point failures and improves response times, enabling safer operations; it may increase training time and wage costs. External nursing adds reliability but introduces coordination challenges with university HR. Fatigue-aware scheduling lowers overtime but relies on accurate sensor proxies and planning.
options: Cross-train technicians across PSG setup, video scoring, and alarm response to ensure seamless coverage during night shifts and reduce risk of skill gaps.
Contract an on-call nursing partner to provide emergency support during peak events and high-risk nights, with predefined escalation protocols.
Institute a data-informed scheduling algorithm that predicts fatigue risk and optimizes guard ratios to sustain safety margins without overstaffing.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: flexibility versus cost and safety margins; weakness: options may not fully address regulatory or union constraints on outside contractors.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to allocate staffing and manage shift coverage

levers.name: Public dissemination and scholarly collaboration plan
levers.consequences: Broadening scholarly engagement accelerates discovery and external validation while preserving privacy; formal data-use agreements reduce legal risk but require ongoing governance. Demonstration events raise visibility but demand careful risk management and budget alignment.
options: Publish quarterly de-identified sensor-aggregate summaries to credentialed researchers, preserving privacy while enabling early scientific discourse.
Develop formal data-use agreements and a transparent access request workflow to accelerate vetted collaborations under strict privacy guardrails.
Host a Bonn-hosted, non-public demonstration day for clinical partners to review methods and discuss potential replication in other sites.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: openness vs privacy; weakness: options may not ensure cross-site reproducibility standards are aligned during early-stage replication.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how to share findings and engage external collaborators

responses.strategic_rationale: The plan seeks to maximize high-quality, longitudinal data on adult NREM parasomnias while maintaining participant safety and privacy in a residential Bonn facility. Tensions exist between delivering rigorous, staged data collection (tiered sensing, enhanced PSG nights) and the practicalities of staffing, cost control, and regulatory oversight. The six levers below target core decision points: how to schedule coverage (execution), how to standardize methods (methodology), how to govern data access (governance), how to optimize event-driven resource use (execution), how to design the living environment for data quality and safety (product), and how to validate results externally (dissemination). Together they balance speed, quality, and compliance to produce publishable new insights and a scalable expansion pathway.

levers.name: Adaptive staffing cadence for nocturnal coverage
levers.consequences: Shifts to an adaptive staffing cadence align nurse-to-participant ratios with observed event yield, improving safety responsiveness and reducing idle overnight labor. This approach can improve data integrity during high-activity nights, but increases scheduling complexity and requires robust cross-training to maintain consistent event adjudication across staff. Staff fatigue management must be integrated to avoid burnout.
options: Implement a rolling 4-week forecast that reallocates night shifts based on live event indicators and predicted data yield from prior nights.
Institute a tiered staffing model where baseline coverage remains stable but surge teams deploy for nights with flagged events or elevated arousal probability.
Pilot a roster modularity scheme with reserve staff who can step in within 60 minutes of a flag, while maintaining documented handoff protocols.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing continuous safety coverage with staff availability and fatigue; the options assume flexible scheduling but do not specify how to handle regulatory constraints or union agreements.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to schedule night shifts to maintain safety and data capture

levers.name: Longitudinal phenotyping protocol harmonization
levers.consequences: Standardizing phenotyping across the study reduces inter-operator variability and improves comparability of event metrics across nights and staff. However, this carries the trade-off of potential delays during the transition period and retraining burdens, with possible data lag until the unified rubric is fully adopted and there may be limited flexibility for atypical cases.
options: Standardize scoring rules across technicians and raters with a concise, site-specific training module and consolidated SOPs.
Adopt a staged harmonization plan that preserves current procedures while gradually integrating a unified scoring rubric across all nights.
Institute a cross-validation protocol where a third expert adjudicates ambiguous episodes during the initial 8 weeks of data collection.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing standardization with site-specific realities; weakness: options don't specify how to measure inter-rater reliability thresholds or handle disagreement beyond two scorers.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — which data collection and scoring approach to harmonize

levers.name: Privacy-preserving data access and synthetic data layer
levers.consequences: Establishes tiered data access and de-identification to comply with GDPR while enabling broader collaboration; synthetic data can support external validation without exposing sensitive material. Trade-offs include potential misalignment between synthetic data properties and real data distributions if not carefully calibrated. Implementation complexity and ongoing governance overhead may increase overall project overhead.
options: Create a multi-tier access scheme with de-identified physiological data released under data use agreements and a separate restricted vault for sensitive video metadata.
Develop synthetic data models that mimic key statistical properties of the dataset to enable external validation without exposing real recordings.
Engage a trusted third-party data steward to audit access requests and enforce privacy-preserving transformations before release.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: enabling collaboration vs strict privacy; weakness: options do not specify audit frequency or how to handle emergency data access.
levers.lever_classification: governance — who may access data and under what conditions

levers.name: Real-time event triage augmentation for targeted PSG nights
levers.consequences: Targeted PSG nights based on real-time triage maximize data yield while limiting participant burden and equipment use. Downstream, escalation thresholds may miss events if too conservative, and may also generate unnecessary resource use if too aggressive.
options: Implement a predefined risk-score threshold to trigger enhanced PSG nights only when flagged by on-site technicians and automated preliminary classifiers.
Adopt a rolling two-night review window to decide whether to escalate to full PSG across the next eligible participant night.
Pilot a decision-log system that records reasons for each escalation to refine future thresholds and reduce unnecessary PSG exposure.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: accuracy gains vs resource consumption; weakness: options assume classifier reliability, but do not address false-negative risk or clinician override procedures.
levers.lever_classification: execution — when to deploy enhanced PSG nights in response to flagged events

levers.name: Participant comfort-first housing design variance
levers.consequences: Integrating domestic design increases participant satisfaction, retention, and natural sleep patterns, improving ecological validity of parasomnia capture. The design choices incur safety and privacy considerations, potential higher renovation costs, and the need for ongoing monitoring to ensure safety does not conflict with the domestic feel.
options: Implement a modular room layout that maintains domestic ambience while enabling rapid privacy and safety adjustments during events.
Introduce a light-touch environmental control system that allows researchers to fine-tune acoustic treatment and lighting to minimize arousal while maintaining realism.
Adopt participant-driven adjustability options for room temperature and bedding to reduce stress and maximize unchanged sleep patterns.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing comfort with safety and privacy; weakness: options do not quantify potential delays or costs of safety upgrades given domestic aesthetics.
levers.lever_classification: product — how the living environment design decisions affect adherence and data quality

levers.name: External validation and replication pathway
levers.consequences: Establishes external collaborations to validate methods and replicate phenotyping, strengthening credibility and enabling funding opportunities. Potential downsides include longer timelines due to partner negotiations and risk of data-sharing constraints that could slow publication.
options: Establish a formal collaboration framework with an independent sleep center to replicate key phenotyping methods on a parallel cohort.
Publish methods and annotation guidelines transparently while preserving privacy by sharing de-identified datasets and scoring protocols.
Schedule periodic cross-site blind scoring exercises to calibrate human raters and monitor inter-rater reliability across sites.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: external validation accelerates impact but introduces negotiation delays; weakness: options do not outline how IP and publication rights are protected during partnerships.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how results are validated externally

levers.name: Tiered sensing escalation protocol
levers.lever_classification: methodology — how to escalate data collection approach
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever directly shapes data richness versus participant burden. An adaptive escalation improves data quality for events but increases scheduling complexity and staffing demands; if escalations fail due to sensor downtime or false negatives, data gaps may arise and equity across participants can be affected.

Implement an adaptive escalation protocol triggered by detected events to allocate full PSG nights only when clinically warranted.

The design choices incur safety and privacy considerations, potential higher renovation costs, and the need for ongoing monitoring to ensure safety does not conflict with the domestic feel.

Introduce a light-touch environmental control system that allows researchers to fine-tune acoustic treatment and lighting to minimize arousal while maintaining realism.
Adopt participant-driven adjustability options for room temperature and bedding to reduce stress and maximize unchanged sleep patterns.
levers.review: Core tension: balancing comfort with safety and privacy; weakness: options do not quantify potential delays or costs of safety upgrades given domestic aesthetics.

levers.name: External validation and replication pathway
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how results are validated externally
levers.consequences: Establishes external collaborations to validate methods and replicate phenotyping, strengthening credibility and enabling funding opportunities. Potential downsides include longer timelines due to partner negotiations and risk of data-sharing constraints that could slow publication.
options: Establish a formal collaboration framework with an independent sleep center to replicate key phenotyping methods on a parallel cohort.
Publish methods and annotation guidelines transparently while preserving privacy by sharing de-identified datasets and scoring protocols.
Schedule periodic cross-site blind scoring exercises to calibrate human raters and monitor inter-rater reliability across sites.
levers.review: Core tension: external validation accelerates impact but introduces negotiation delays; weakness: options do not outline how IP and publication rights are protected during partnerships.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Classification**
   - `lever_classification`: a brief phrase with the category and what the lever decides.
   - Format: "category — what this lever decides".
   - Categories: methodology, execution, governance, dissemination, product, operations.
   - Examples: "governance — who oversees the review process", "methodology — which data collection approach to use", "execution — how to sequence the rollout phases".

3. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

4. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

5. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

6. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

7. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

2026-Mar-11

Project start ASAP

File 'plan_type.md':
This plan requires one or more physical locations. It cannot be executed digitally.

**Explanation:** This plan *unequivocally requires* a physical location (the research unit in Bonn, Germany), physical renovations, physical equipment, in-person staffing, and physical participants. The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
